metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether the teardown cleanup retry loop can reuse `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (or `_call_with_backoff` if it gets wired into the live path by C4) before specifying a new bespoke implementation; if reuse is blocked, document the blocking reason.
findings.finding: The teardown-failure fallback specifies a new bespoke '3x exponential-backoff retry loop' for cleanup calls without acknowledging that two existing backoff implementations already live in the codebase: `_call_with_backoff` (acli_subprocess.py:236-292, full exp-backoff + jitter + Retry-After for urllib HTTP errors) and `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py:100-144, exp-backoff for JiraAPIError/429/5xx). Building a third ad-hoc implementation violates the Rule-of-Three and risks divergent behaviour and maintenance burden.
findings.location: Harness section — Teardown-failure fallback: 'each cleanup call retries 3x with exponential backoff'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Teardown-failure fallback section, replace 'each cleanup call retries 3x with exponential backoff' with 'each cleanup call retries via `dispatch_one._call_with_retry(client.delete_issue, key, max_retries=3)` (the existing production backoff wrapper), falling back to `leaked-artifacts.log` on `RetryExhaustedError`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the blast-radius signal definition exactly how the harness must invoke `classify.census()` — it requires both `total_bindings=<N>` and `max_acting_fraction=<fraction>` kwargs for the `'breaker'` key to be present; update the assertion example to reflect the correct call signature.
findings.finding: `census()['breaker']` only appears when `census()` is called with BOTH `max_acting_fraction` and `total_bindings` kwargs (classify.py:368: `if max_acting_fraction is not None and total_bindings is not None`). The plan's NOTE says the key appears 'when the breaker is evaluated on an acting pass,' but never specifies HOW the harness invokes census() with those arguments. A call to `classify.census(decisions)` without those kwargs will always return a dict without 'breaker', causing the assertion `census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False` to raise KeyError.
findings.location: Signal definitions / Blast-radius breaker section; classify.py:342-376
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Assert via the breaker API in classify.py: census()["breaker"]["allowed"] == False' with: 'Call `classify.census(decisions, total_bindings=N, max_acting_fraction=0.10)` and assert `result["breaker"]["allowed"] == False`; or call `classify.check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings=N, max_acting_fraction=0.10)` and assert `verdict.allowed == False`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the correct monkey-patch target(s) for C4: since apply_inbound/apply_outbound/applier import `_call_with_retry` by name from batch_dispatch, the patch must be applied at each consuming module (e.g., `patch.object(apply_inbound, '_call_with_retry', ...)`) OR the C4 test must patch `_run_acli` in `acli_subprocess` instead (module-qualified calls make that viable).
findings.finding: `_call_with_retry` is defined in `dispatch_one.py` but re-exported via `batch_dispatch.py`, and consumed from `batch_dispatch` by `apply_inbound.py`, `apply_outbound.py`, and `applier.py`. Monkey-patching `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (or `batch_dispatch._call_with_retry`) will NOT intercept calls in modules that have already imported the symbol by reference (e.g., `from rebar_reconciler.batch_dispatch import _call_with_retry` in apply_inbound.py:27 binds the name locally). The C4 signal definition names `_call_with_retry (dispatch_one.py)` as the monkey-patch target without acknowledging this multi-import-path issue.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C4 section; dispatch_one.py:100; apply_inbound.py:27; apply_outbound.py:26; applier.py:178

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the blast-radius harness call must be `classify.census(decisions, total_bindings=N, max_acting_fraction=F)` with both keyword arguments, so the `'breaker'` key is present and `census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False` is a valid assertion rather than a KeyError.
findings.finding: `census()['breaker']` is only present when `max_acting_fraction` AND `total_bindings` are both passed as keyword arguments to `census()` (classify.py:368); the plan asserts `census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False` as the blast-radius validation but never specifies that the harness must call `census(decisions, total_bindings=N, max_acting_fraction=F)` with those params — a bare `census(decisions)` call omits the 'breaker' key entirely and the assertion will KeyError.
findings.location: Signal definitions / Blast-radius breaker section; classify.py:368
findings.suggested_fix: In the blast-radius signal definition, add: 'Call `census(decisions, total_bindings=len(synthetic_bindings), max_acting_fraction=0.10)` — both keyword args are required for the `breaker` key to appear in the result dict (classify.py:368).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'if suitable' conditional: document that `e2e_validation_probe.sh` requires a `trap cleanup EXIT` addition (and `set +e` in the trap body) to meet the teardown guarantee, OR commit to the pytest `yield`+`finally` path — the current probe is NOT suitable as-is for guaranteed bilateral cleanup.
findings.finding: The plan proposes 'build on the existing `e2e_validation_probe.sh` if suitable' for guaranteed bilateral teardown, but the existing probe uses `set -euo pipefail` with Phase 6 cleanup as inline code — there is no `trap cleanup EXIT`. Any non-zero exit from Phases 1-5 causes the script to abort immediately under `set -e`, leaving Jira and local artifacts un-cleaned. Building on this script as-is is structurally incompatible with the plan's own requirement of teardown 'even on assertion failure/exception'.
findings.location: Harness section; scripts/jira-pressure-test/e2e_validation_probe.sh lines 22, 451-475
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add `trap 'cleanup_all_artifacts' EXIT` near the top of the probe with `set +e` inside the cleanup function, disabling pipefail for the cleanup body; or (b) use the pytest autouse fixture path, which provides `yield`+`finally` semantics natively.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `census(decisions, total_bindings=N, max_acting_fraction=0.10)` call signature the harness must use so the `'breaker'` key is guaranteed present in the return dict.
findings.finding: The blast-radius assertion `census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False` will raise a KeyError at runtime because `classify.census()` only adds the `'breaker'` key to its return dict when BOTH `max_acting_fraction` AND `total_bindings` kwargs are supplied (classify.py:368: `if max_acting_fraction is not None and total_bindings is not None`). The plan never specifies what argument values the harness must pass to `census()` to trigger breaker evaluation — leaving the executor without a processing protocol for invoking the API correctly.
findings.location: Signal definitions / Blast-radius breaker signal; classify.py:342-376
findings.suggested_fix: In the blast-radius signal definition, add: 'Call `classify.census(decisions, total_bindings=<synthetic_binding_count>, max_acting_fraction=0.10)` — both kwargs are required; omitting either means `census()` returns no `"breaker"` key.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the teardown-failure fallback to reuse `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (or `_call_with_retry` re-exported via `batch_dispatch`) for the 3-attempt cleanup retry rather than specifying a new inline backoff loop.
findings.finding: The Harness teardown-failure fallback specifies a new bespoke '3x exponential-backoff' retry loop for cleanup calls, but `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` already implements exactly this (exp backoff on 429/5xx, configurable max_retries) with 15+ production call-sites in src/. Adding another inline retry loop is NIH.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Teardown-failure fallback: each cleanup call retries 3x with exponential backoff'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'each cleanup call retries 3x with exponential backoff' with 'each cleanup call is wrapped in `_call_with_retry(client.delete_issue, id, max_retries=3)` — the same production utility used for all other Jira mutations, already tested for 429/5xx/TimeoutError handling.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the C3 signal baseline from 'today only stderr' to 'today completely silent (no signal)' to match the actual current behavior confirmed in the C3 ticket and apply_outbound.py.
findings.finding: The C3 signal definition states 'today only stderr' as the current baseline for the field-allowlist-drop alert, but reading the C3 ticket (nonglacial-tense-vulture) and apply_outbound.py confirms the drop is COMPLETELY SILENT ('with no signal — the only place the reconciler degrades without telling anyone'); no stderr write exists in apply_outbound.py either. A harness author who reads 'today only stderr' will write a pre-C3 baseline assertion that looks for existing stderr output, which will pass vacuously or fail for the wrong reason.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C3 paragraph: 'today only stderr — assert the post-C3 alert'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'today only stderr — assert the post-C3 alert' to 'today completely silent (no alert, no stderr) — assert the post-C3 BRIDGE_ALERT fires on a dropped outbound field'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the C4 AC to require that the 429-injection test wraps a real live Jira operation (e.g., a real `create_issue` or `update_issue` call to the test project), so the retry loop exercises the actual live boundary, not just in-process logic.
findings.finding: The C4 validation scenario as described — 'monkey-patch the LIVE retry path ... a simulated 429 exit -> backoff -> success' — is compatible with a pure offline/unit test that injects a fake 429 at the subprocess level without ever making a real Jira call. Within a harness that is otherwise explicitly live, the AC for C4 does not require the underlying operation to be a real live Jira request, so the suite could go green while the actual end-to-end retry+live-Jira path remains unexercised.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C4 section; AC line: 'One observable pass criterion per matrix scenario (C1-C4 ...)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit AC clause: 'The C4 backoff test MUST use a real Jira API call (against the test project) as the underlying operation; the monkey-patch injects the 429 at the first attempt only, and success is confirmed by a real Jira response on retry. Purely offline/subprocess-stub-only C4 tests do not satisfy this criterion.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the C4 Retry-After capability assertion before the harness is written: confirm whether C4 (lambswool-shaded-eider) adds Retry-After parsing to `_run_acli` (subprocess path) or only to `_call_with_retry`/`_call_with_backoff`, and update the C4 signal definition to name the specific patching target and the exact assertion (jitter-only vs. Retry-After-aware).
findings.finding: The C4 signal asserts 'Retry-After honoring only IF C4 confirmed availability', implying the live `_run_acli` path can receive and act on the Retry-After HTTP header. This is an unverified capability assertion: `_run_acli` (acli_subprocess.py:337-444) operates on subprocess `CalledProcessError` exit codes with a fixed `time.sleep(2**(attempt+1))` — it has no access to HTTP headers at all. The only implementation with Retry-After awareness is `_call_with_backoff` (acli_subprocess.py:267), which reads `exc.headers.get('Retry-After')` from `urllib.error.HTTPError` — but `_call_with_backoff` is dead code with zero production call sites. The plan neither resolves whether C4 actually adds Retry-After awareness to `_run_acli` nor distinguishes which 'live retry path' to patch.
findings.location: Signal definitions — C4: 'Retry-After honoring only IF C4 confirmed availability'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the coupling between the harness and private symbols (`_run_acli`, `_call_with_retry`, `acli.py:721-723`, `ProvenanceLedger`) and either (a) propose a thin interface/hook these signals validate through, or (b) add a maintenance note in the ADR that these harness references must be updated in lockstep with C-band implementation.
findings.finding: The harness spec hardcodes internal implementation symbols (`_run_acli`, `_call_with_retry`, `_call_with_backoff`, `acli.py:721-723`, `ProvenanceLedger` in `conflict_resolver.py`) without acknowledging the coupling risk or proposing a stable interface boundary — if C4 or other C-band tickets rename/move these during implementation, the harness breaks silently with no mitigation path.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C4 section; Harness / Setup probe section
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR/design note, add a section 'Harness coupling points' listing each private symbol the harness directly references, with a note that the harness owner must verify these on each C-band merge. For C4 specifically, consider a thin test-hook (e.g., an injectable retry-counter) rather than monkey-patching the private function name directly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the C4 patch-target ambiguity with a co-located decision rule: specify exactly which function to patch (e.g., 'patch `acli_subprocess._run_acli` after C4 lands its 429 branch; if C4 probe shows ACLI retries 429 internally, patch `_call_with_retry` instead') so the executor has an unambiguous protocol.
findings.finding: The C4 signal definition names two structurally incompatible monkey-patch targets (`_run_acli` subprocess retry loop in `acli_subprocess.py` and `_call_with_retry` in `dispatch_one.py`) without a co-located decision rule for which to patch. These operate in different error domains: `_run_acli` retries `subprocess.CalledProcessError` (no 429 HTTP code concept pre-C4), while `_call_with_retry` retries `JiraAPIError` with `.status_code`. C4's own ticket (lambswool-shaded-eider) confirms 429 handling in `_run_acli` is FUTURE work (pre-manifest). The plan provides no protocol for resolving this ambiguity post-C4-land.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C4 (429/backoff) signal; acli_subprocess.py:337-444; dispatch_one.py:100-144

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a wall-clock timeout for the harness overall (e.g., pytest `--timeout=N` or CI job timeout) AND per-API-call timeouts so that a total Jira outage triggers a controlled failure, reaches teardown, and logs to `leaked-artifacts.log` rather than hanging indefinitely.
findings.finding: No wall-clock timeout is specified for the harness as a whole or for individual Jira API calls in the test body. If Jira becomes unresponsive mid-run (not a 429, but a full outage or hang), the C4 backoff path does not apply, teardown may never be reached, and CI blocks indefinitely.
findings.location: Harness section / Signal definitions (C4) / AC: 'Runs after the C-band lands; re-runnable'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `timeout=30` (or equivalent) to each Jira API call in the harness; set a global pytest timeout (e.g., `--timeout=300`); document both in the harness design note / ADR so they are maintained.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'a named path' with a concrete relative path (e.g. `test-results/rebar-e2e-report.xml`) so the AC can be verified with `ls test-results/rebar-e2e-report.xml && jq -e . test-results/rebar-e2e-report.xml`.
findings.finding: The JSON/JUnit report output path is described as 'a named path' but no name is given, making the AC unverifiable — a reviewer cannot confirm the artifact exists at the right location.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'writes a JSON/JUnit report to a named path attached to the ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'writes a JUnit XML report to `test-results/rebar-e2e-report.xml` (CI artifact path)' and add a proving command: `xmllint --noout test-results/rebar-e2e-report.xml && grep -c 'testcase' test-results/rebar-e2e-report.xml`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note' with a specific observable outcome, e.g. 'ADR added at `docs/adr/0042-e2e-harness-pattern.md` covering teardown strategy, harness type choice, and isolation approach; verifiable with `ls docs/adr/0042-e2e-harness-pattern.md`'.
findings.finding: The AC 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note' is effort-framed (produce a document) rather than stating a specific observable outcome with a verification command, violating F1's observable-outcome requirement.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last bullet: 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan-body section (or expand 'Harness') that describes the ADR/design note: what decisions it records, where it lives (repo path or wiki), and who authors it — so AC6's 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note' clause has covered, scoped work behind it.
findings.finding: AC6 contains an orphan deliverable — 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note' — that has no corresponding described work anywhere in the plan body (Why, Harness, Signal definitions, or Isolation sections). No section describes who produces the ADR, what it contains, or when it is written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6: 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Design note' subsection under Harness: 'Author `docs/adr/NNN-e2e-validation-harness.md` recording: (a) harness pattern choice (pytest autouse vs bash trap), (b) bilateral-teardown guarantee mechanism, (c) credential/isolation model. Attach path to the ticket report artifact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the C4 Retry-After conditional is resolved at harness runtime: e.g., an env var `C4_RETRY_AFTER_AVAILABLE=1/0` set from the C4 probe result, or a named field/tag on the C4 ticket that the harness reads before deciding whether to assert the Retry-After honor branch.
findings.finding: The C4 signal specifies asserting 'Retry-After honoring only IF C4 confirmed availability' — but the plan is the validation harness that runs AFTER C4 lands. The Retry-After availability is explicitly marked UNVERIFIED in the C4 ticket itself ('ACLI is a subprocess, so Retry-After availability is UNVERIFIED'). The harness plan does not specify how it will access the C4 probe result (which ticket field/comment records the outcome) to conditionally enable the Retry-After assertion branch, leaving the conditional assertion unanchored.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C4 section; lambswool-shaded-eider ticket description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly which `mutation_count` field the harness reads for the echo-suppression idempotency check (e.g., `reconcile_once()` return dict `'mutation_count'` key, filtered to field-update mutations only) and how the baseline pre-seeding maps to that counter.
findings.finding: The echo-suppression signal asserts `mutation_count==0` on the next pass over just-written data, but `mutation_count` is a field that appears in at least three structurally distinct locations in the codebase (`pass_io.py` pass record, `applier.py` return dict at line 682, `reconcile.py` result dict at line 1166) and can mean different things (local field mutations vs. all mutations including Jira calls). The plan never specifies which `mutation_count` field the harness should read, nor where to read it from, creating an ambiguous state-increment / assertion target.
findings.location: Signal definitions / Echo suppression signal; pass_io.py:47-63; applier.py:610,682; reconcile.py:1166

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the C4 Retry-After branch explicitly: either 'if C4 probe confirms Retry-After available: assert header is honored (proving command X); else: emit a `SKIP: retry-after-unavailable` line in the test report' — so the harness output proves which branch ran.
findings.finding: The C4 Retry-After assertion is explicitly conditional ('assert...Retry-After honoring only IF C4 confirmed availability'), with no stated fallback proving command when Retry-After is unavailable. This makes the C4 AC partially conditional — it is satisfiable without exercising the Retry-After code path, and there is no documented criterion that distinguishes a 'Retry-After not available' outcome from a 'Retry-After check was skipped/broken' outcome.
findings.location: Signal definitions — C4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC5, explicitly fix the TOMBSTONE grace-period count alongside the oscillation count — e.g. 'TOMBSTONE grace N=`RECONCILER_ABSENT_RETIRE_GRACE`=3 consecutive 404s; oscillation N=3 passes' — or use distinct variable names (N_osc, N_grace) in both Signal definitions and AC5 to eliminate ambiguity.
findings.finding: 'N' is overloaded across two distinct concepts in the plan without disambiguation in AC5: the status-round-trip signal uses 'N=3 passes' for oscillation detection, while TOMBSTONE/GRACE uses 'N=`RECONCILER_ABSENT_RETIRE_GRACE` (default 3)' for consecutive-404 retirement. AC5 pins only the oscillation meaning ('N (oscillation passes) = 3'), leaving the TOMBSTONE N implicitly pinned only by the env-var default — not by the AC.
findings.location: Signal definitions — 'Status round-trip' and 'TOMBSTONE/GRACE'; Acceptance Criteria — AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Rename: Signal definitions → 'assert no oscillation over N_osc=3 passes' and 'N_grace=`RECONCILER_ABSENT_RETIRE_GRACE` (default 3)'; AC5 → 'N_osc (oscillation passes)=3; N_grace (TOMBSTONE 404s)=3 (env `RECONCILER_ABSENT_RETIRE_GRACE`)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit teardown step for `bindings-retired.json`: either use a separate throwaway file path for the test run (isolated from the real file) or record the binding keys written during the TOMBSTONE/GRACE scenario and remove exactly those entries during bilateral cleanup.
findings.finding: `bindings-retired.json` entries written during the TOMBSTONE/GRACE scenario have no defined cleanup path in teardown. The harness's bilateral teardown tracks synthetic Jira issues and the local env, but a retirement entry appended to `bindings-retired.json` by the grace-period logic persists after the run, polluting production or shared state.
findings.location: Signal definitions — TOMBSTONE/GRACE / Harness (guaranteed bilateral teardown) / AC: 'ZERO surviving artifacts on success'
findings.suggested_fix: Configure the harness to point `BINDINGS_RETIRED_PATH` (or equivalent env var) at a temp file for the test run, so teardown simply deletes the temp file rather than surgically editing the shared file. Document this isolation in the ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a runtime guard (e.g., inspect `acli_subprocess._run_acli` source or a version sentinel) in the C4 scenario that skips/xfails with a clear message if C4's jittered-backoff implementation is not yet present, preventing a vacuous green on a pre-C4 run.
findings.finding: The C4 signal definition says to patch `_run_acli` (acli_subprocess.py) as the preferred target for simulating a 429 subprocess exit. `_run_acli` is called module-qualified (`acli_subprocess._run_acli`) from `acli.py` and `acli_cli_ops.py` — making it a viable patch target. However, C4 (lambswool-shaded-eider / 943f-8303-829b-443a) is pre-manifest and its AC requires the probe to run FIRST (AC1: 'Probe recorded') before the implementation lands. The plan says to 'assert the JITTERED-BACKOFF behavior C4 lands' — but if C4 hasn't landed yet, `_run_acli` won't have jittered-backoff logic to exercise; patching it to simulate a 429 would test current (no-jitter) retry behavior. This is an out-of-scope behavioral dependency the plan acknowledges ('runs AFTER the C-band lands') but does not sequence carefully for C4.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C4 section; lambswool-shaded-eider (943f-8303-829b-443a) status: open/pre-manifest

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture of `leaked-artifacts.log`: confirm it contains only bare artifact IDs (strip ACLI error verbosity before appending), specify its write location (outside default artifact upload globs or scoped to admins-only download), and document its retention policy.
findings.finding: The `leaked-artifacts.log` CI artifact introduced by the teardown-failure fallback has an undeclared access posture: the plan does not specify the file's write location, its content bounds (bare artifact IDs only vs. full ACLI error response verbosity), or who can read it as a CI artifact. ACLI teardown error responses can include HTTP headers and auth-challenge context beyond bare IDs, and CI artifacts are typically readable by all repository collaborators.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Teardown-failure fallback: ... append the id to leaked-artifacts.log (a CI artifact)'
findings.suggested_fix: Append only the artifact ID string (not raw ACLI stderr) to leaked-artifacts.log; write it to a dedicated path (e.g., `test-results/leaked-artifacts.log`); in the CI job, upload it with an explicit `if: failure()` and restrict artifact visibility to admins if the CI platform supports it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the harness reads the breaker fraction threshold from the same config/env source as production (rather than hardcoding `0.09`) so the blast-radius test stays correct if the threshold is tuned.
findings.finding: The blast-radius scenario hardcodes the breaker fraction `0.09` and the synthetic-binding count `>=11` directly in the plan (and these will presumably flow into the harness as literals), but these are tied to a production threshold that may be configurable — if the production breaker threshold changes, the test silently becomes wrong (either stops triggering the breaker or always trips it).
findings.location: Signal definitions / Blast-radius breaker section
findings.suggested_fix: In the harness setup, import or read the breaker threshold constant from `classify.py`/config (e.g., `from classify import BREAKER_FRACTION_THRESHOLD`) and derive the synthetic binding count as `ceil(1 / BREAKER_FRACTION_THRESHOLD) + 1` rather than literals `11` and `0.09`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific secrets-management mechanism for the Jira service-account token (e.g. GitHub Actions encrypted secret, HashiCorp Vault, AWS SSM) and add a note on rotation/audit so the injection contract is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan instructs that the Jira service-account token must be 'injected via secret, never logged/committed' but never names the concrete secrets-management mechanism (e.g. GitHub Actions encrypted secrets, HashiCorp Vault, AWS SSM Parameter Store). Without a named store, an implementer has no contract for how to provision, rotate, or audit the credential, leaving the door open to ad-hoc solutions (env file checked in, unencrypted CI variable, etc.).
findings.location: Isolation, credentials section + AC line 4: 'credential scoped to the test project + injected via secret'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Isolation, credentials' section add one sentence, e.g.: 'The token is stored as a GitHub Actions encrypted secret (`JIRA_TEST_TOKEN`) — never in a .env file or CI plain-text variable — and rotated on the project's standard 90-day schedule.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the exact secrets-injection mechanism for the harness API token (e.g., 'inject via GitHub Actions `${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}`; env-var only, never a CLI arg, never written to disk') in the Isolation/credentials section so the credential access posture on the Jira boundary-crossing path is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan's credential handling for the harness states 'token via a secret, never logged/committed' but never declares the concrete injection mechanism (e.g., GitHub Actions `${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}`, environment variable sourced from a vault, CI secret injection). The access posture of the credential on the boundary-crossing path is therefore undeclared — an agent running the harness locally vs. in CI would inject the token differently, with no specified guardrail against CLI-arg exposure, temp-file writes, or subprocess env dumps.
findings.location: Isolation, credentials section — 'token via a secret, never logged/committed'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Isolation, credentials' section: 'Token injected via GitHub Actions encrypted secret (`${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}`), env-var only — never passed as a CLI argument, never written to disk or a config file.' This mirrors the already-established pattern in reconcile-bridge.yml:89 and closes the posture declaration gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify concurrent-write safety for `leaked-artifacts.log`: either use a per-run namespaced filename (e.g., `leaked-artifacts-$RUN_ID.log`) as the CI artifact, or document that parallel runs against the same project are prohibited and enforce it via CI concurrency groups.
findings.finding: `leaked-artifacts.log` is written to by append on teardown exhaustion, but no concurrent-write safety is specified. If multiple CI runs (e.g., parallel matrix jobs) execute simultaneously against the same Jira project and both exhaust teardown retries, concurrent appends could corrupt or interleave entries, making the log unreliable for incident recovery.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Teardown-failure fallback' / AC: 'Teardown-failure fallback (3x exp-backoff, leaked-artifacts.log CI artifact, non-zero exit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a per-run log path such as `leaked-artifacts-${GITHUB_RUN_ID}-${GITHUB_JOB}.log` so each CI run writes its own artifact; CI collects all matching files as artifacts. This also makes the isolation guarantee explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the harness-type choice in the plan itself (e.g. 'Use pytest autouse fixture with yield+finally as the primary pattern; fall back to bash trap only if the suite cannot import the test client') so the executor has a stated default rather than an open 'if suitable' branch.
findings.finding: The harness implementation choice is deferred to the executor with no resolution criteria: 'Build on the existing e2e_validation_probe.sh if suitable; else a pytest autouse fixture' — 'if suitable' is not a decision, it is an open design question handed to the agent.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Build on the existing e2e_validation_probe.sh if suitable; else a pytest autouse fixture'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Build on the existing e2e_validation_probe.sh if suitable; else a pytest autouse fixture' with a decided default: 'Use a pytest autouse session-scoped fixture with yield+finally (preferred); if the harness must remain pure-bash, use trap cleanup EXIT with a $CREATED_IDS array.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a setup step that reads C1's manifest at harness-init time and asserts the discovered field matches a documented invariant (e.g. field key prefix or schema shape), so the proving command for C1 is 'harness init log shows field_key=<discovered>; assertion target recorded in test output' rather than fully implicit.
findings.finding: C1's observable pass criterion is deliberately left undefined at plan time ('C1 is pre-manifest; do NOT hard-code an unverified field'), yet the AC asserts 'one observable pass criterion per matrix scenario (C1–C4...)'. The C1 assertion can only be written after C1 lands — there is no pre-specified proving command, so the AC cannot be evaluated until runtime discovery completes.
findings.location: Signal definitions — C1; Acceptance Criteria line 2

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
